ncert:biology:11:11:11.title: Photosynthesis in Higher Plants
ncert:biology:11:11:11.summary: This section introduces photosynthesis as a vital physico-chemical process where green plants convert light energy into chemical energy to synthesize organic compounds. It emphasizes its role as the primary source of food on Earth and the generator of atmospheric oxygen.

ncert:biology:11:11:11.1.title: What do we Know?
ncert:biology:11:11:11.1.summary: This section introduces the fundamental requirements for photosynthesis, which include chlorophyll, light, and carbon dioxide. It describes classic experiments, such as starch testing on variegated leaves and KOH-treated setups, to demonstrate the necessity of these components.

ncert:biology:11:11:11.10.title: Factors affecting Photosynthesis
ncert:biology:11:11:11.10.summary: This section explores the internal and external factors that regulate the rate of photosynthesis in plants. It highlights Blackman's Law of Limiting Factors, which states that the rate of a multi-factor biochemical process is governed by the factor closest to its minimum level.

ncert:biology:11:11:11.10.1.title: Light
ncert:biology:11:11:11.10.1.summary: This section explores how light intensity affects the rate of photosynthesis, demonstrating a linear relationship at low intensities that plateaus as other factors become limiting. It defines the light saturation point and explains how excessive light beyond this point can cause chlorophyll breakdown, reducing photosynthesis.

ncert:biology:11:11:11.10.4.title: Water
ncert:biology:11:11:11.10.4.summary: This section describes how water acts as a limiting factor in photosynthesis, primarily through indirect physiological effects. It explains how water stress leads to stomatal closure and leaf wilting, which subsequently reduce carbon dioxide availability and light absorption.

ncert:biology:11:11:11.2.title: Early Experiments
ncert:biology:11:11:11.2.summary: This section traces the historical milestones and key experiments that led to our understanding of photosynthesis. It highlights the contributions of scientists like Priestley, Ingenhousz, Sachs, Engelmann, and van Niel, culminating in the correct balanced chemical equation for photosynthesis.

ncert:biology:11:11:11.3.title: Where does Photosynthesis take Place?
ncert:biology:11:11:11.3.summary: This section identifies the chloroplasts within mesophyll cells as the site of photosynthesis and details their internal structure. It explains the division of labor within the chloroplast, distinguishing between light-driven reactions in the membrane system and carbon-fixing dark reactions in the stroma.

ncert:biology:11:11:11.4.title: How many Types of Pigments are Involved in Photosynthesis?
ncert:biology:11:11:11.4.summary: This section explains how leaf pigments can be separated using paper chromatography and identifies chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, xanthophylls, and carotenoids. It describes the relationship between light absorption and photosynthetic rate using absorption and action spectra, highlighting chlorophyll a as the primary pigment. Finally, it discusses how accessory pigments assist in light absorption and protect chloro...

ncert:biology:11:11:11.6.3.title: Chemiosmotic Hypothesis
ncert:biology:11:11:11.6.3.summary: This section explains the chemiosmotic hypothesis, which describes how ATP is synthesized in chloroplasts via a proton gradient across the thylakoid membrane. It details the factors contributing to the gradient—water splitting, proton transport by plastoquinone, and NADP reductase activity—and how ATP synthase utilizes this gradient to produce ATP.

ncert:biology:11:12:12.0.title: Introduction to Cellular Respiration
ncert:biology:11:12:12.0.summary: This section introduces cellular respiration as the process of breaking down food materials within cells to release and trap energy as ATP. It contrasts autotrophic and heterotrophic energy acquisition and defines respiratory substrates like carbohydrates, proteins, and fats.

ncert:biology:11:12:12.1.title: Do Plants Breathe?
ncert:biology:11:12:12.1.summary: This section explains how plants perform gaseous exchange without specialized respiratory organs, utilizing stomata, lenticels, and loose parenchyma cells. It introduces the chemical equation of respiration, where glucose is catabolized to release energy, and discusses anaerobic respiration and glycolysis as evolutionary adaptations.

ncert:chemistry:11:2:2.3.4.title: Evidence for the Quantized Electronic Energy Levels: Atomic Spectra
ncert:chemistry:11:2:2.3.4.summary: This section explains atomic spectra as key evidence for quantized electronic energy levels, distinguishing between continuous, emission, and absorption spectra. It introduces spectroscopy and details the line spectrum of hydrogen, characterized by the Balmer series and the generalized Rydberg formula.

ncert:physics:12:12:12.3.title: Atomic Spectra
ncert:physics:12:12:12.3.summary: This section introduces atomic spectra, detailing how excited atomic gases emit specific wavelengths to form emission line spectra. It also explains absorption spectra, which occur when white light passing through a gas produces dark lines at those same characteristic wavelengths.

relationship_policy.hard_dependencies: DEPENDS_ON_UNIT edges must affect ordering; to_section_id should appear before from_section_id.
relationship_policy.required_concepts: REQUIRES_CONCEPT explains prerequisite warnings and checkpoint questions.
relationship_policy.teaches_concepts: TEACHES_CONCEPT explains concept coverage and can reveal downstream next steps.
relationship_policy.transfer_support: TRANSFER_SUPPORTS_UNIT is an optional bridge, not mandatory curriculum sequence.
relationship_policy.related_by_concept: RELATED_BY_CONCEPT is reinforcement or comparison only, not a prerequisite.
relationship_policy.next_steps: Next-step paths are suggested after completion, not before the main module.
